lblHazard: Slippery, dirty or unclear surface (Locks µ to 0.2)
modalSubTitle: Tons of cargo per top-over strap (LC=1600, Stf=400 daN at steep angle)
optRiskTippingSideways: The risk of cargo tipping sideways
optRiskTippingLong: The risk of cargo tipping forward or backward
resTippingDominates: ⚠️ Tipping risk dominates: The geometry of the cargo means that the risk of stabling/tipping requires more straps than friction sliding ({maxTipping} pcs vs {maxSliding} pcs).
resSlidingDominates: ✅ Sliding risk dominates: Friction is the decisive component. The risk of sliding requires more straps than the dimensions' tipping risk.
expDirectTitle: Direct lashing is based on mechanical strength (LC) and angles:
expDirectStep1:
Movement requirement minus friction: Requirements according to standard are 80% forward, 50% backward/sideways. Remaining force after friction deduction:
Forward: 80% - {muPercent}% = {remPercent}% of weight
expDirectStep2:
Angular loss: Calculated with standard geometry based on selected angle type:
Effective strength per strap: approx {capStrap} tons
expDirectStep3: Symmetry: Always rounded up to the nearest even pair so that the cargo is not pulled sideways.
expTopOverTitle: Why does it require {total} straps for {weightKg} kg of cargo?
expTopOverStep1:
Bed friction deduction: Braking requires securing for 80% of the weight forward. Since the bed friction helps with {muPercent}%, only {remPercent}% remains to be secured mechanically:
Force left to secure forward = {weightKg} kg × {remDecimal} = {remForceKg} kg
expTopOverStep2:
Connection to the TYA table: The industry reference table is based on a standard strap (Stf = 400 daN) deployed at a steep angle. At friction µ = {mu}, it gives a base forward capacity of:
Forward capacity (Base): {baseVal} tons ({baseValKg} kg) / strap
expTopOverStep3:
Adjustment for your marking (Stf): With your input value of {stf} daN, the strap's basic effect is:
{stf} daN ÷ 400 daN = {factor}x effect per strap
expTopOverStep4:
Angle check according to the TYA rule: You have selected {angleRangeText}. {angleExplText}
Real capacity per strap forward = {baseVal} tons × {factor} {angleMath} = {trueCap} tons ({trueCapKg} kg)
expTopOverStep5: Final calculation & norm: The remaining need divided by your strap's real strength ({remForceKg} kg ÷ {trueCapKg} kg/strap = {exactStraps} straps) mathematically requires {mathStraps} straps. But according to the TYA norm, free-standing cargo must be secured with at least 2 straps to guarantee stability!
lblTippingWarningTitle: 💡 Smart lashing tip under tipping risk:
lblTippingWarningDesc: Since the securing is governed by tipping risk rather than sliding risk, standard top-over lashings are ineffective. Consider using a loop lashing around the top of the cargo, blocking forward, or using direct lashing to drastically reduce the number of straps and increase stability.
lblSteelTipDesc: Steel on steel or steel on a trailer bed slides very easily. Do not place heavy machinery or steel cages directly on the deck. Instead, place them on a standard wooden pallet or use wooden battens. By changing the contact surface to wood-to-plywood, you increase friction to 0.45 instantly and avoid drawing half the straps!
lblSoftCargoTipDesc: You are securing cargo that can deform (cardboard/plastic). If you tension the strap hard, the cargo will compress, causing the strap to become slack and lose all tension after just a few kilometers. Place corner protectors along the upper edges to distribute pressure!
lblBlockingTipDesc:
The cargo is heavy but very stable, so it will not tip. What requires so many straps is the high sliding force forward during hard braking.

👉 Block the cargo forward: Place the pallet directly against the front headboard or use empty pallets as spacers forward. Blocking the forward movement mechanically can often cut the required top-over lashings in half!
lblRingsTipDesc: You have chosen a steep lashing angle, providing maximum downward force. Remember: when tensioning straps with full force over heavy cargo, the combined vertical force on the trailer's lashing rings becomes enormous. Make sure your lashing points are rated for the weight!
lblStandardTipDesc: Since the cargo is stable and sliding risk dominates, top-over lashing is an excellent choice. Just make sure to place the straps as close to a 90° angle to the bed as possible for maximum friction effect!
